issues.description: The new test asserts `event_id is not None`, but `EventStore.emit()` is declared `-> None` and contains no return statement (see orchestrator/src/orchestrator/event_store.py:97-133). At runtime `event_id` will always be `None`, so this assertion will fail every time the test is run. The test is verifying behavior that does not exist in the production code. None of the existing call sites (scheduler.py, harness.py, merge_queue.py, steward.py, workflow.py) consume a return value either, so adding this test in isolation does not match any in-flight production change.
issues.suggested_fix: Either (a) modify `EventStore.emit()` to capture `cursor = conn.execute(...)` and `return cursor.lastrowid`, change its return annotation to `int | None` (None on the fire-and-forget failure path), and update the docstring; or (b) drop this test entirely until emit() is changed. If you keep the test, also assert uniqueness (`event_id_1 != event_id_2` across two emits) since the docstring claims to verify a 'unique' identifier.

issues.description: `event_id = store.emit(...)` captures the return value but never asserts on it. Since `emit()` returns `None`, this is dead code today. If the intent was to verify the return value, an assertion is missing; if not, the assignment should be removed. Combined with the new test, this looks like a half-finished refactor where the production change was forgotten.
issues.suggested_fix: Either drop the assignment (`store.emit(...)`) or add an explicit assertion (`assert event_id is not None and isinstance(event_id, int)`) after also implementing the production-side return.

issues.description: Original assertions (`len(rows) == 1` / `row = rows[0]`) were strict and correct: each test creates a fresh `tmp_path`, so exactly one row is expected. Loosening to `len(rows) >= 1` and filtering by task_id masks regressions where emit() might write multiple rows or stray rows for one logical call. The strict equality should be preserved unless the production behavior is actually changing to write multiple rows per emit (which the diff does not indicate).
issues.suggested_fix: Revert lines 34-35 to `assert len(rows) == 1` and `row = rows[0]`. The filter-by-task-id pattern is unnecessary in an isolated tmp_path test and weakens coverage.

issues.description: The assertions are sloppy: `any(True for r in rows if r['task_id'] == '99')` is an awkward idiom (the `True` is dead — `any(r['task_id'] == '99' for r in rows)` is the standard form), and the redundant `len(rows) > 0` adds nothing if `any()` already passed. The docstring promises to verify a 'unique' identifier, but the test only emits a single event and never compares two ids. Coverage is shallow and partly redundant.
issues.suggested_fix: Replace with: `assert len(rows) == 1`, `assert rows[0]['task_id'] == '99'`, and emit a second event to assert `event_id_2 != event_id_1` and matches `rows[1]['id']`. Or, if uniqueness is not the intent, fix the docstring to match what is actually checked.

issues.description: The new test re-creates the same `db_path = tmp_path / 'test.db'` + `EventStore(db_path, '...')` boilerplate that already appears in five other tests in this file. A small pytest fixture (e.g., `@pytest.fixture def store(tmp_path) -> tuple[EventStore, Path]`) would consolidate this and make the test set easier to extend.
issues.suggested_fix: Introduce a `store_and_path` fixture that yields `(EventStore(tmp_path / 'test.db', 'run-id'), tmp_path / 'test.db')` and refactor the existing tests to use it. Optional/cleanup, not required for correctness.

issues.description: If you do change `emit()` to return `cursor.lastrowid`, remember the failure path: `emit()` is documented as fire-and-forget and the except clause swallows errors. The function must still return something (e.g., `None`) on the failure path so the contract is `int | None`. Callers that adopt the return value will need to tolerate `None`, otherwise the fire-and-forget guarantee silently breaks.
issues.suggested_fix: If implementing the return: `cursor = conn.execute(...); conn.commit(); return cursor.lastrowid` inside the try, leave the implicit `return None` on the except path, annotate as `-> int | None`, and update the docstring to call out the None-on-failure contract. Add a regression test that simulates an emit failure (similar to `test_fire_and_forget_bad_path`) and asserts the return is `None`.

comprehensive_reviewer.summary: The diff is a broken half-refactor: it modifies tests to consume a return value from `EventStore.emit()`, but `emit()` is declared `-> None` and contains no return statement (event_store.py:107-133). The new `test_emit_returns_event_id` will fail at runtime on `assert event_id is not None`, since `emit()` always returns `None`. The accompanying changes to `test_emit_writes_event` weaken its assertions (`len(rows) == 1` → `len(rows) >= 1`, plus a now-unused `event_id` capture) for no apparent reason — the test uses an isolated tmp_path so the strict count was correct. The new test's assertions are also sloppy (`any(True for ...)`, redundant length check, no uniqueness check despite the docstring claim). To unblock: either implement `emit()` returning `cursor.lastrowid` with `int | None` annotation and a None-on-failure contract, or drop the new test. Either way, restore the strict assertions in `test_emit_writes_event` and either use or remove the captured `event_id` variable.
